raw_consumer_statement:
I filed a billing dispute with American Express regarding an XXXX charge in the amount of {$3900.00}. In my initial dispute submission, I uploaded the maximum number of evidentiary files allowed by American Express ( XXXX files ) through the dispute portal. These materials included photographs, screenshots, and written documentation supporting the dispute. 

American Express denied the dispute based on XXXX findings : ( XXXX ) that I did not notify the merchant within XXXX hours of check-in, and ( XXXX ) that a partial refund resolved the dispute. Both findings were consistent with the merchants response but inconsistent with the evidence I submitted. 

After receiving the denial, I submitted a written rebuttal explaining that I had already submitted the maximum number of evidentiary files, that the evidence was already in American Expresss possession, and that the denial did not reflect review of those materials. I requested reconsideration and correction of the factual errors identified in the denial. 

American Express subsequently issued a second denial that repeated the same XXXX findings without addressing the rebuttal and without reflecting consideration of the evidence submitted with the initial dispute. 

As a result, American Express appears to have relied exclusively on the merchants assertions and failed to conduct a good-faith review of the cardmember-submitted evidence within its own dispute process constraints.
